content.definition: Low cardiac output syndrome is inadequate cardiac pump performance after cardiac surgery causing insufficient oxygen delivery. A common definition is CI below 2.0-2.2 L/min/m2 with hypoperfusion or significant inotrope need.

content.text: LCOS is a syndrome, not a final diagnosis. Causes include myocardial stunning after bypass, ischaemia, poor LV function, RV failure, valve dysfunction, tamponade, hypovolaemia, arrhythmias, excessive afterload and vasoplegia.

items: Check arterial line level, zero and damping before acting on pressure alone.
Assess rhythm first: AF, complete heart block, junctional rhythm or VT may be the cause.
Assess preload using bleeding, drains, CVP/PAOP trend, echo, pulse pressure and fluid responsiveness.
Assess LV/RV contractility, valve function, ischaemia and tamponade.
Assess afterload: high SVR limiting LV ejection, low SVR from vasoplegia or high PVR affecting RV.
Escalate rising lactate, CI below 2.2 L/min/m2, oliguria, cool peripheries, low SvO2 or increasing support.

content.text: Definition and physiology

LCOS means oxygen delivery is inadequate for tissue demand because cardiac pump flow is insufficient. Oxygen delivery depends on cardiac output, haemoglobin and arterial oxygen saturation. When flow falls, SvO2 falls, lactate rises, urine output falls and organ dysfunction follows. MAP alone does not exclude low flow.
Why it occurs after cardiac surgery

Bypass, cross-clamp, cardioplegia and reperfusion can cause myocardial stunning. The heart may be oedematous, acidotic, ischaemic or recovering from long bypass time. Risk is higher with pre-existing LV dysfunction, hypertrophy, pulmonary hypertension, renal disease, urgent surgery and prolonged bypass.
Major causes

rows: Low CI after bypass with potential improvement.
Graft failure, spasm, embolism or coronary compromise
ST change, arrhythmia, regional wall-motion abnormality.
Low output after valve surgery; echo diagnosis.
High PVR, RV infarct, hypoxia, acidosis, PEEP
High CVP, low LV preload, hypotension.
High CVP, narrow pulse pressure, low output.
Low filling pressures, high variation, low Hb.
Low output improves with rhythm/pacing correction.
Low MAP with normal/high CO possible.

content.text: Vasoplegia versus true pump failure

Vasoplegia causes hypotension from low SVR, often with normal or high CI. True pump failure causes low CI, poor stroke volume and high extraction. Mixed shock is common. Giving only inotrope to vasoplegia may worsen tachycardia; giving only vasopressor to pump failure may raise afterload and reduce output.
Clinical and haemodynamic signs

LCOS presents with narrow pulse pressure, cool peripheries, mottling, delayed capillary refill, falling urine output below 0.5 mL/kg/hr, rising lactate above 2 mmol/L, low SvO2 below 60 percent, metabolic acidosis, altered mentation, low CI and increasing vasoactive support.
Treatment principles

items: Optimise oxygen delivery by correcting SpO2, Hb, pH, temperature, K, Mg, calcium, pain and shivering.
Optimise preload using small reassessed boluses, blood products for bleeding, or diuresis only when overloaded.
Improve contractility with inotropes such as adrenaline, dobutamine, milrinone or levosimendan according to ventricular side, BP, SVR and PVR.
Use vasopressors such as noradrenaline or vasopressin for vasoplegia and coronary/organ perfusion pressure.
Reduce afterload only when high afterload is limiting LV ejection and BP can tolerate it.
Optimise rhythm and pacing, especially AV sequential pacing after valve surgery or with diastolic dysfunction.
Escalate for IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO or RV support when pharmacological therapy cannot maintain perfusion.

content.text: LCOS is not an answer. Ask: rhythm, preload, pump, afterload, oxygen delivery, obstruction or bleeding?
A normal MAP on high-dose noradrenaline can hide low cardiac index.
AV sequential pacing can be a haemodynamic treatment.
Ventilator settings affect RV failure: hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis and high PEEP increase PVR.
Do not treat all post-bypass hypotension as pump failure; vasoplegia may have normal or high cardiac output.
ECG and monitor setup

For low cardiac output syndrome, the ECG monitor must be treated as a clinical device, not wallpaper. Use a lead that shows atrial activity when P waves matter, print strips during events, check electrode contact, and repeat a 12-lead when rhythm diagnosis, ischaemia or conduction disease is uncertain.
Arterial waveform correlation

The arterial line determines whether electrical activity is producing mechanical output. Rate, rhythm and monitor labels are secondary to MAP, pulse pressure, waveform amplitude, lactate, urine output and clinical perfusion.
Defibrillator and pacing readiness

LCOS requires accurate haemodynamic monitoring, pacing optimisation and readiness for mechanical support requires early access to pads, synchronised cardioversion, defibrillation or pacing depending on stability. In post-cardiac surgery patients, also check epicardial wires, pacing box battery, output, sensitivity, capture and sensing.

rows: Lead quality, rhythm strip, alarm limits and artefact.
Avoids missed rhythm change or false diagnosis.
Conduction, QRS width, ischaemia and baseline comparison.
Clarifies rhythm and identifies dangerous mimics.
Level, zero, damping, pulse pressure and waveform presence.
Pads, sync markers, battery and energy pathway.
Mode, rate, output, sensitivity and capture threshold.
Essential when rate or AV synchrony fails.
Aim above 2.2 L/min/m2; severe concern below 2.0
Interpret with lactate, urine output and SvO2.
At least 65 mmHg; often 70-80 mmHg or surgical target
Falling trend; concern above 2 and rising; major concern 4 or above
Low output for 2 hours is concerning.
Suggests high extraction from low delivery.
Often at least 65-70 percent if used
Trend more useful than isolated value.
About 1.10-1.30 mmol/L depending on local range
Low calcium worsens contractility and vasoplegia.
Hypothermia worsens bradycardia/coagulopathy; fever raises demand.

content.text: Escalate falling urine output, narrowing pulse pressure, increasing support, low SvO2 or lactate above 2 and rising.
Escalate urgently for CI below 2.2 with hypoperfusion, suspected RV failure, tamponade, valve dysfunction, ischaemia or arrhythmia-driven LCOS.
Activate emergency response for profound shock, loss of arterial waveform, rapidly rising lactate with collapse or need for emergency mechanical support/theatre.

quiz.question: What CI commonly defines LCOS with hypoperfusion?
quiz.explanation: LCOS commonly uses CI below 2.0-2.2 with hypoperfusion markers.

quiz.question: Why can vasoplegia mimic pump failure?
options: MAP is low despite normal or high CO because SVR is low.
quiz.explanation: Vasoplegia is low SVR shock and requires vasopressor strategy.

quiz.question: Which pattern suggests tamponade causing LCOS?
options: High CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, falling MAP and low urine output.
Bounding pulse and low CVP only.
Normal pulse pressure and no symptoms.
quiz.explanation: Tamponade causes high pressure but low effective preload and low output.
